rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR The Purpose Of Posting The Tentative Ruling
rulings.Rulings: AMENDED TENTATIVE [Part 2 of 2] Also before the court is Moving Respondents' Motion to Strike. The motion to strike is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. It is granted as to the allegations against Moving Respondent Mejia. Petitioner added Mejia as a respondent and the allegations against her well after the responsive pleadings were filed and without prior court authorization and the additions were not within the scope of the court's grant of leave to amend. In all other respects, the motion is denied. The court notes that if the allegations of the SAP are proven and absent any other relevant evidence, a reasonable trier of fact could make the bad-faith finding contemplated by Probate Code section 859. The court has considered all papers filed through September 22, 2020. Petitioner is ordered to lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on September 23, 2020. Remote appearance is strongly encouraged, in conformity with the current General Order of the Probate and Mental Health Division, available on the court's website at https://sfsuperiorcourt.org/divisions/probate. [Part 2 of 2]

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Respondents Kilby Stenkamp, Adelaida Mejia, And Vanguard Properties, Inc. To Strike Portions Of Second Amended Petition; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: AMENDED TENTATIVE [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Joseph M. Quinn: Before the court is Respondents Vanguard Properties, Inc., Kilby Stenkamp and Adelaida Mejia's Requests for Judicial Notice filed June 30, 2020. These requests for judicial notice are GRANTED. (See Evid. Code, section 452, subds. (c), (d) & (h).) Notice is limited to the relevant facts properly subject to notice given the materials presented and does not, for example, extend to the truth of assertions in pleadings or transcripts. Moving Respondents also filed Supplemental Requests for Judicial Notice on June 30, 2020. These requests, which were submitted with the reply brief, are untimely and DENIED. Also before the court is Moving Respondents' Demurrer to the Second Amended Petition ("SAP"). The demurrer is OVERRULED. The issue is assuming true the facts properly pled and facts properly noticed, does the SAP necessarily fail to state a claim for relief against the Moving Respondents. (See Code Civ. Proc., section 430.10(e); Blank v. Kirwan (1985) 39 Cal.3d 311, 318.) "[I]f, on consideration of all facts stated, it appears that plaintiff is entitled to any relief against defendant, the complaint will be held good, though facts may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statement of other facts irrelevant to cause of action shown, or though plaintiff may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged." (Augustine v. Trucco (1954) 124 Cal.App.2d 229, 236, quoting Matteson v. Wagoner (1905) 147 Cal. 739, 742.) Here, Petitioner's claim for relief against the Moving Respondents survives demurrer. In his Probate Code section 850 petition, Petitioner essentially alleges that decedent died having a claim to real property known as 64 Potomac and the property or some interest therein is claimed by others, including Moving Respondents, or decedent died having a claim to 64 Potomac, title to or possession is claimed to belong to others, including Moving Respondents. (See Prob. C., section 850, subds. (a)(2)(C)&(D).) It is true that the allegations of the SAP may not be clearly stated, or may be intermingled with statements of irrelevant facts and that Petitioner may demand relief to which he is not entitled under facts alleged. But none of this means that the SAP fails entirely as against the Moving Respondents. (See Augustine, supra, 124 Cal.App.2d at p. 236.) Moving Respondents ask the court to rule on the demurrer based on facts beyond those alleged or noticeable. This the court cannot do. For example, Moving Respondents ask the court to take notice of a certificate of trust to show they did not actually know that Non-moving Respondent Lawrence Porchia was acting without authority. But whether Moving Respondents even saw-let alone reasonably relied on-the certificate is a fact beyond the SAP and not subject to notice. Thus, whether Moving Respondents are entitled to the protections afforded by Probate Code sections 18100 and 18100.5 cannot be decided on this demurrer. [Part 1 of 2]
